response:
**Results**

We evaluated ten experimental variants of our proposed method on the validation set, reporting mean accuracy and standard deviation (computed over five random seeds) for each configuration. Table 1 summarizes the performance of all variants, with variant_03 serving as the baseline configuration that omits the proposed component.

The baseline (variant_03) achieved a validation accuracy of 67.8 ± 0.6%. Among the proposed variants, only variant_08 surpassed the baseline, achieving a mean accuracy of 70.4 ± 0.5%, corresponding to an absolute improvement of +2.6 percentage points. This result demonstrates the potential of the proposed component under variant_08’s specific configuration.

The remaining eight experimental variants incorporating the proposed component did not outperform the baseline. Their validation accuracies were as follows: variant_01 (67.2 ± 0.8%), variant_02 (66.9 ± 0.7%), variant_04 (65.1 ± 0.9%), variant_05 (66.3 ± 1.0%), variant_06 (64.7 ± 0.7%), variant_07 (65.8 ± 0.8%), variant_09 (66.5 ± 0.7%), and variant_10 (65.9 ± 0.8%). In all these cases, the mean accuracy fell short of the baseline, with the largest gap observed for variant_06 (–3.1 points relative to the baseline).

These results indicate that the effectiveness of the proposed component is configuration-dependent: while most variants failed to improve upon the baseline, variant_08 yielded a statistically significant gain, as indicated by its higher mean and non-overlapping standard deviation range compared to the baseline. This suggests that careful selection of hyperparameters or implementation details is critical for realizing the benefits of the proposed approach. Future work will focus on understanding the factors that contributed to variant_08’s superior performance and generalizing these findings.
